chief_complaint: I think I have genital warts

note.soap:
S:  
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of skin-colored bumps and some ulcers located on her genital area. She reports mild itching associated with these lesions, which she notes actually improves with scratching. She has not tried any treatments so far. She recently had a new sexual partner and engaged in unprotected intercourse. She has no known medication allergies except penicillin. She is not pregnant and has no history of renal or liver disease.

O:  
Phone consultation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Genital warts (condyloma acuminata) caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection. The presentation of skin-colored bumps with mild itching in the genital area, along with recent unprotected sexual exposure, supports this diagnosis.  
- Differential:  
  1. Herpes simplex virus infection (though ulcers are present, the chronicity and lack of pain make this less likely)  
  2. Molluscum contagiosum  
  3. Syphilitic chancre or condyloma lata (secondary syphilis)  
  4. Other sexually transmitted infections causing genital lesions (e.g., chancroid)  
- Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms, no severe pain, no bleeding, no signs of acute infection or systemic illness.

P:  
- Disposition: Patient is stable for outpatient management with follow-up as needed.  
- Plan: Educate the patient about the likely diagnosis of genital warts caused by HPV. Discuss the natural history of the condition and the potential for spontaneous resolution. Recommend evaluation by a healthcare provider for physical examination and confirmation of diagnosis. Treatment options include topical agents such as imiquimod, podophyllotoxin, or cryotherapy, which can be initiated after clinical confirmation. Counsel on safe sexual practices and the importance of condom use to reduce transmission risk. Screen for other STIs given recent unprotected sex and new partner.  
- Safety net: Advise the patient to seek urgent care if lesions become painful, rapidly increase in size or number, develop signs of secondary infection (redness, swelling, discharge), or if she develops systemic symptoms such as fever. Recommend follow-up for further evaluation and treatment planning.

evidence.title: Human Papillomavirus (HPV) Infection - STI Treatment Guidelines
evidence.snippet: ect genital warts and tests for other STIs. No recommendations can be made regarding informing future sex partners about a diagnosis of genital warts because the duration of viral persistence after warts have resolved is unknown. Special Considerations Pregnancy Podofilox, podop…

evidence.title: Genital warts - NHS
evidence.snippet: gnancy. But you'll usually be referred to a sexual health specialist if you have genital warts and you’re pregnant. During pregnancy, genital warts may: multiply and get bigger (they may be removed if they're very big) become more easily irritated be passed on to the baby during…
